question: How do I join the office hours or live workshop if the Zoom link isn’t shared with students?
answer_llm:
You don’t join via the Zoom link as a student — the Zoom link is only shared with instructors/presenters/TAs.

For students, the session is available through:
- **YouTube Live** on the **DataTalksClub YouTube channel**
- The **video URL** posted in the **announcements channel on Telegram and Slack** before it starts
- **Slido** for questions, with the link pinned in the live chat

If you’re stuck, you can also ask in **Slack** and follow the course’s question-asking guidelines.
answer_orig:
The zoom link is only published to instructors/presenters/TAs.

Students participate via YouTube Live and submit questions to Slido (link is pinned in the chat when live). The video URL should be posted in the [announcements channel on Telegram and Slack](https://t.me/dezoomcamp) before it begins. You can also watch live on the DataTalksClub [YouTube Channel](https://www.youtube.com/c/DataTalksClub).

Don’t post questions in chat as they may be missed if the room is very active.

question: How do I find my entry on the leaderboard if I can’t spot my name?
answer_llm: Click the **“Jump to your record on the leaderboard”** link. Your account is automatically assigned a random display name, so that link will take you directly to your entry.
answer_orig:
When you set up your account, you are automatically assigned a random name, such as “Lucid Elbakyan.” Click on the "Jump to your record on the leaderboard" link to find your entry.

If you want to see what your Display name is, click on the "Edit Course Profile" button.

<{IMAGE:image_1}>

- **First field:** This is your nickname/displayed name. You can change it if you want to be known by your Slack username, GitHub username, or any other nickname of your choice. This is useful if you want to remain anonymous.
- **Second field:** Change this to your official name as in your identification documents—passport, national ID card, driver's license, etc. This is mandatory if you do not want "Lucid Elbakyan" on your certificate. This name will appear on your Certificate!

question: Why does the leaderboard show a random name for me instead of my real one?
answer_llm:
The leaderboard shows your separate **public display name**, not your certificate name. That display name is **assigned randomly by default** (for example, “Lucid Elbakyan”) so your real name stays off the public leaderboard.

If you want to change it, go to **Edit Course Profile**:
- **First field:** public nickname / leaderboard name
- **Second field:** official name for your certificate

question: Why does the homework content keep changing before the deadline?
answer_llm:
Because the homework may still be in draft mode. If the homework title contains `[DRAFT]`, it means it isn’t ready yet, so the content can still change.

The homework is only considered ready when:

- the homework form is open on the course management platform, and
- the homework title does **not** contain `[DRAFT]`.

Until then, changes are expected, and working on it early is at your own risk.
answer_orig:
If the homework title contains `[DRAFT]`, it means the homework is not ready yet.

The homework is ready only when both are true:

- The homework form is open on the course management platform.
- The homework title does not contain `[DRAFT]`.

Until then, the content can still change. Working on the material or homework in advance is at your own risk, because the final version can be different.

question: Why do I get an OpenAI API error about insufficient credits when I run responses.create?
answer_llm:
You get that error because your OpenAI API account has **insufficient credits / quota**. The course notes say there is **no free usage** of the OpenAI API, so you need to **add funds** to your account.

To fix it:
1. Add credits to your OpenAI account (minimum $5).
2. Make sure you’re using a model available to your account in `responses.create(...)`.
3. If needed, create a new API key after adding credits and update it locally.

If you want, I can also help you distinguish this from a 429 rate-limit error.
answer_orig:
You may receive the following error when running the OpenAI `responses.create` command due to insufficient credits in your OpenAI account:

```
OpenAI API Error: Insufficient credits
```

question: What does the "Insufficient credits" message mean in OpenAI responses.create?

question: Why am I getting a 429 RateLimitError from OpenAI even though my code looks fine?
answer_llm:
Because a 429 from OpenAI is often **not** a code bug — it can mean **you’ve run out of API quota/credits**.

From the context, the specific error:

- `RateLimitError: Error code: 429`
- with `type: 'insufficient_quota'`

means **billing/quota issue**, not just “too many requests.”

What to do:
1. **Add billing / credits** to your OpenAI account.
2. **Use a model available to your account**.
3. You may need to **create a new API key** after enabling billing.
4. If you’re making many requests in a loop, also reduce call frequency and use retries/backoff.

If the error says `insufficient_quota`, that’s the key clue: **your account ran out of quota**, even if the code itself looks fine.

question: Why does the course use uv instead of pip and virtualenv?
answer_llm:
The course uses **uv** because it’s **fast and convenient**. It’s an all-in-one tool that can replace **pip**, **virtualenv**, and several other Python tools, so instead of managing multiple tools you can handle Python versions, virtual environments, and dependencies in one place with reproducible lockfiles.

You **don’t have to use uv** — **pip and virtualenv still work fine** — but uv makes the setup simpler and faster, which is why the lessons use it.
answer_orig:
The course uses uv because it's fast and convenient. It's an all-in-one tool that replaces pip, virtualenv, pip-tools, pipx, poetry, pyenv, and twine — instead of juggling several tools, you manage Python versions, virtual environments, and dependencies through one fast command with reproducible lockfiles.

You're not required to use it — pip and virtualenv still work fine — but uv makes the setup simpler and faster, which is why the lessons use it.

Official docs: https://docs.astral.sh/uv/

question: Is uv really replacing tools like pip, virtualenv, poetry, and pyenv?
answer_llm:
Yes — **uv is meant to replace a bunch of separate Python tools** like `pip`, `virtualenv`, `pip-tools`, `pipx`, `poetry`, `pyenv`, and `twine` by handling Python versions, virtual environments, and dependency management in one fast tool.

That said, **it’s not mandatory**. `pip` and `virtualenv` still work fine; `uv` is just simpler and faster, which is why the course uses it.

question: What do I need to set up before I can call Gemini from the Module 1 notebook using the OpenAI-style endpoint?
answer_llm:
You need three things before calling Gemini from the Module 1 notebook via the OpenAI-style endpoint:

1. A Gemini API key saved in your `.env` file, for example `GEMINI_API_KEY`
2. An OpenAI client configured with Google’s OpenAI-compatible base URL
3. The Gemini model name you want to use in the request

For example:

```python
client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GEMINI_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai/"
)
```

I can also show the exact notebook code change if you want.
answer_orig:
To get started you need three things:

1. A Gemini API key saved in your `.env` file, for example as `GEMINI_API_KEY`.
2. An OpenAI client pointed at Google’s OpenAI-compatible base URL.
3. Your selected Google Gemini model name in your request.

Example code (loads the API key from `.env`, creates the Gemini client, and defines the `llm` helper):

```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv
from openai import OpenAI

load_dotenv()

client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GEMINI_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai/"
)

def llm(instructions, user_prompt, model="gemini-3.1-flash-lite"):
    message_history = [
        {"role": "developer", "content": instructions},
        {"role": "user", "content": user_prompt}
    ]

    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model=model,
        messages=message_history
    )

    return response.choices[0].message.content
```

This uses the older chat completions style via the OpenAI-compatible endpoint, whereas many course examples use the newer Responses format. That means you will need to change the notebook code in a few places, especially where it reads the model response and where it handles tools or function calls.

question: Why do the course examples need changes when I use Gemini with chat completions instead of the newer Responses API?
answer_llm:
Because Gemini with the OpenAI-compatible **chat completions** endpoint returns results in a different shape than the newer OpenAI **Responses API**.

In the course examples, code often expects things like:

- `response.output`
- `response.output_text`
- Responses-style tool calls / parsing

But with Gemini via `chat.completions.create(...)`, you usually need to read:

```python
response.choices[0].message.content
```

and sometimes adjust how tools are passed in as well.

So the examples need changes because:

1. **The API surface is different**  
   Responses API and Chat Completions use different response fields.

2. **Gemini’s OpenAI-compatible endpoint is chat-completions-oriented**  
   It works better with the older `chat.completions` flow.

3. **Tools/function calls may need adaptation**  
   The tool schema may not match the exact shape the course code expects for Responses API.

In short: the notebook code was written for the newer Responses format, but Gemini’s compatible endpoint follows the chat-completions format, so the response handling and tool handling must be updated.

question: How do I get Pydantic structured output from Gemini if `responses.parse` isn’t supported through the OpenAI-compatible endpoint?
answer_llm:
Use the OpenAI SDK’s **chat completions parsing flow** instead of `responses.parse`.

Define a Pydantic model, then call:

```python
response = client.chat.completions.parse(
    model="gemini-3.1-flash-lite",
    messages=messages,
    response_format=Questions
)
```

Then read the parsed object from:

```python
result = response.choices[0].message.parsed
```

So the key idea is: **stay on the OpenAI SDK, but use `chat.completions.parse` with Gemini’s OpenAI-compatible endpoint**.
answer_orig:
To get parsed structured output, use the OpenAI SDK's chat-completions parsing flow instead of the newer Responses API. This is the right choice when you want to stay on the OpenAI SDK but call a chat-completions-compatible model like Gemini through the OpenAI-compatible endpoint.

First, define the structure you want Gemini to return as a Pydantic model:

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel

class Question(BaseModel):
    question: str
    answer: str

class Questions(BaseModel):
    questions: list[Question]
```

Then call `chat.completions.parse` with `response_format=Questions`. Pass the same kind of `messages` you would to any chat completion:

```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv
from openai import OpenAI

load_dotenv()

client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GEMINI_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai/"
)

messages = [
    {"role": "developer", "content": "Generate three FAQ questions about RAG."},
    {"role": "user", "content": "Topic: structured output with Gemini"}
]

response = client.chat.completions.parse(
    model="gemini-3.1-flash-lite",
    messages=messages,
    response_format=Questions
)

result = response.choices[0].message.parsed
print(result.questions)
```

Notes:
- This approach keeps using the OpenAI SDK while leveraging Gemini through Google's endpoint.
- The parsed output (e.g., `result.questions`) is a Pydantic object available directly, with no manual JSON parsing.
- `chat.completions.parse` requires `pydantic` (`uv add pydantic`).

question: Why is `chat.completions.parse` the recommended way to handle parsed FAQ output with Gemini on the OpenAI-style endpoint?
answer_llm:
`chat.completions.parse` is recommended because, with Gemini on the OpenAI-compatible endpoint, it lets you stay in the OpenAI SDK’s chat-completions flow while getting **directly parsed Pydantic objects** back.

In other words:

- `responses.parse` is not the right API here for non-OpenAI providers.
- `chat.completions.parse` works with Gemini through the OpenAI-style endpoint.
- It returns structured output in `response.choices[0].message.parsed`, so you don’t need to manually JSON-parse the FAQ output.
- It fits the course pattern of using `chat.completions` for providers like Gemini that support that interface better than the newer Responses API.

So the main reason is: **it gives you structured FAQ output cleanly and natively, without manual parsing, while matching Gemini’s OpenAI-compatible chat-completions endpoint.**
answer_orig:
To get parsed structured output, use the OpenAI SDK's chat-completions parsing flow instead of the newer Responses API. This is the right choice when you want to stay on the OpenAI SDK but call a chat-completions-compatible model like Gemini through the OpenAI-compatible endpoint.

First, define the structure you want Gemini to return as a Pydantic model:

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel

class Question(BaseModel):
    question: str
    answer: str

class Questions(BaseModel):
    questions: list[Question]
```

Then call `chat.completions.parse` with `response_format=Questions`. Pass the same kind of `messages` you would to any chat completion:

```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv
from openai import OpenAI

load_dotenv()

client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GEMINI_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai/"
)

messages = [
    {"role": "developer", "content": "Generate three FAQ questions about RAG."},
    {"role": "user", "content": "Topic: structured output with Gemini"}
]

response = client.chat.completions.parse(
    model="gemini-3.1-flash-lite",
    messages=messages,
    response_format=Questions
)

result = response.choices[0].message.parsed
print(result.questions)
```

Notes:
- This approach keeps using the OpenAI SDK while leveraging Gemini through Google's endpoint.
- The parsed output (e.g., `result.questions`) is a Pydantic object available directly, with no manual JSON parsing.
- `chat.completions.parse` requires `pydantic` (`uv add pydantic`).

question: How do I install Ollama on Mac, Windows, or Linux?
answer_llm:
To install Ollama:

- **macOS**: Go to [https://ollama.com/download](https://ollama.com/download), download the **`.pkg`** file, and install it.
- **Windows**: Go to [https://ollama.com/download](https://ollama.com/download), download the **`.msi`** file, and install it.
- **Linux**: Run this command in your terminal:

```bash
curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh
```

After installation, you can test it by running:

```bash
ollama run llama3
```

question: Why does tiktoken say one number, but OpenAI’s usage shows a few more tokens for the same chat prompt?
answer_llm:
`tiktoken` is usually counting only the visible text you pass in, while OpenAI’s API counts the full chat formatting too.

So the API total can be a few tokens higher because it includes:

- about **4 structural tokens per message** in `chat.completions.create()`
- about **2 extra tokens overall** to start assistant generation
- sometimes a bit more for **whitespace, newlines, or unusual Unicode**

That’s why `tiktoken` might show one number, but OpenAI reports a slightly larger one for the same prompt.
answer_orig:
When using `tiktoken.encode()` to count tokens in your prompt, you might see a difference compared to OpenAI’s API response. For instance, you might get 320 tokens, while OpenAI reports 327. This is due to internal tokens added by OpenAI’s chat formatting.

Here’s what happens:

- Each message in a `chat.completions.create()` call (e.g., `{role: "user", content: "..."}`) adds 4 structural tokens (role, content, separators).
- The API adds 2 tokens globally to mark the start of assistant response generation.
- Extra newlines, whitespace, or uncommon Unicode characters in your content may slightly increase the token count.

Thus, even if your visible text is 320 tokens, OpenAI may count 327 due to these internal additions.

question: Where do the extra tokens come from in a chat.completions request, even when my text looks shorter?
answer_llm:
They come from OpenAI’s internal chat formatting, not just your visible text.

In a `chat.completions.create()` request:

- **Each message adds 4 structural tokens** for things like role/content separators.
- **The API adds 2 extra tokens globally** to mark the start of assistant generation.
- **Whitespace, newlines, or unusual Unicode** can add a few more.

So even if your prompt text seems shorter, the API can report more tokens because of those hidden formatting tokens.

question: How can I keep my OpenAI API key loaded automatically instead of exporting it in every new terminal?
answer_llm:
Use `dirdotenv` so the key loads automatically when you `cd` into the project:

```bash
uv tool install dirdotenv

# add to ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc:
eval "$(dirdotenv hook bash)"   # or zsh
```

Then, in your project:

```bash
echo 'OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...' > .env
echo '.env' >> .gitignore
```

After that, you don’t need to export the key in every new terminal; it will be loaded automatically for that directory.

If you already use it, `direnv` is also fine.
answer_orig:
Use [`dirdotenv`](https://github.com/alexeygrigorev/dirdotenv). It is like `direnv`, but works with both `.env` and `.envrc`, and is more portable across shells and operating systems.

```bash
uv tool install dirdotenv

# add this line to your ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc:
eval "$(dirdotenv hook bash)"   # or zsh

# inside your project:
echo 'OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...' > .env
echo '.env' >> .gitignore
```

After that, the key is loaded automatically when you `cd` into the project directory.

Important: always add `.env` and `.envrc` to `.gitignore` so keys never land on GitHub.

`direnv` is also fine if you already use it. In that case, create `.envrc`, add your exports there, and run `direnv allow`.

For GitHub Codespaces, use the built-in [Codespaces secrets](https://docs.github.com/en/codespaces/managing-your-codespaces/managing-your-account-specific-secrets-for-github-codespaces) instead of files in the repo.

For Python scripts, the equivalent is `python-dotenv`:

```python
from dotenv import load_dotenv
load_dotenv()  # loads .env from project root
```

question: How do I decide on field weights in minsearch without guessing too much?
answer_llm:
The systematic way is to **evaluate different weight settings against a ground-truth dataset** rather than guessing.

A practical approach:
1. Create a small set of representative questions.
2. Mark which documents should be retrieved for each question.
3. Try different field weights in `minsearch`.
4. Compare retrieval metrics like:
   - hit rate
   - precision@k
   - recall@k
   - MRR

question: What’s the best way to clean documents before using them in RAG?
answer_llm:
The best way is to make the documents **clean, structured, and easy to chunk and retrieve**.

Common steps:
- Remove obvious noise: broken HTML, duplicate text, boilerplate, OCR errors, repeated headers/footers
- Preserve useful context: titles, section names, dates, page numbers, speaker names, Q&A structure
- Store the result in a structured format that’s easy to process, often JSON
- Chunk the documents so related context stays together
- Keep metadata that may help filtering or ranking later

The goal is not just to shorten the text, but to make retrieval more accurate.

question: In GitHub Codespaces, `uv` prints “Failed to hardlink files” — is that actually a problem or can I ignore it?
answer_llm:
No — it’s usually not a real error in GitHub Codespaces.

`uv` may print **“Failed to hardlink files”** because it can’t hardlink between its cache and the target environment in Codespaces. The install still works; `uv` just falls back to **copying files**.

If you want to hide the warning in the current shell:

```bash
export UV_LINK_MODE=copy
```

To make it persistent:

```bash
echo 'export UV_LINK_MODE=copy' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc
```
answer_orig:
No. This warning can happen in GitHub Codespaces when `uv` cannot hardlink files between the cache and the target environment.

The package still installs. `uv` falls back to copying files.

To suppress the warning for the current shell:

```bash
export UV_LINK_MODE=copy
```

To make it persistent:

```bash
echo 'export UV_LINK_MODE=copy' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc
```

See the `uv` documentation for more details: [https://docs.astral.sh/uv/](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/).

question: Why does WSL2 say the model needs more memory even though my PC has enough RAM?
answer_llm:
Because WSL2 does **not use all of your PC’s RAM by default**. It only has access to the amount of memory allocated to WSL2, which may be much smaller than your total system RAM.

In the course context, the fix is to increase WSL2’s memory allocation by creating a `.wslconfig` file in:

`C:\Users\YourUsername\.wslconfig`

with something like:

```ini
[wsl2]
memory=8GB
```

Then restart WSL:

```bash
wsl --shutdown
```

After that, you can check the available memory inside WSL with `free`.
answer_orig:
Your WSL2 is set to use Y.Y GiB, not all your computer memory. To allocate more RAM, follow these steps:

1. Create a `.wslconfig` file under your Windows user profile directory: `C:\Users\YourUsername\.wslconfig`.

2. Include the desired RAM allocation in the file:

   ```ini
   [wsl2]
   memory=8GB
   ```

3. Restart WSL using the command:

   ```bash
   wsl --shutdown
   ```

4. Run the `free` command in WSL to verify the changes.

For more details, read [this article](https://www.aleksandrhovhannisyan.com/blog/limiting-memory-usage-in-wsl-2/).

- Go to [github.com/codespaces](https://github.com/codespaces), stop the codespace, and start it again.
- If it still won't connect, open it in the **browser** instead of desktop VS Code, or try a different browser (Edge/Chrome/Brave).
- As a last resort, delete the codespace and create a new one.

**"My setup is all gone":**

- The repo in `/workspaces` persists across stop/start, but a **rebuild or a brand-new codespace** starts from a clean image, and system/global installs outside your project don't always survive. Reinstalling is quick with `uv` (`uv sync` / `uv add ...`).
- **Commit and push your work often** — uncommitted changes survive a stop/start but are lost if you delete or recreate the codespace.

If Codespaces keeps being flaky for you, consider running the course locally instead — see "[Can I run the course locally instead of Codespaces?](026_aa310de435_can-i-run-the-course-locally-instead-of-codespaces.md)".

question: Can I use a different LLM provider instead of OpenAI in the RAG module, or am I stuck with the course default?
answer_llm:
Yes — you’re **not stuck with OpenAI**. The course explicitly says you can use **any LLM provider** in the RAG module.

Good options include:

- **OpenAI-compatible hosted providers** like Groq, OpenRouter, DeepSeek, Gemini, Z.ai, Mistral
- **Open-source models on Hugging Face**
- **Local models** via Ollama, vLLM, LM Studio, etc.
- A **GPU machine** serving a model remotely
- Even a **VPN** if the provider is blocked in your region

Usually, if the provider is OpenAI-compatible, you only need to change:

- `base_url`
- API key
- model name

So yes, you can use a different provider instead of OpenAI.
answer_orig:
If the provider used in the course isn't available or is blocked in your region (or you simply prefer another one), you can use any other LLM provider — the course isn't tied to OpenAI. Just switch to something else:

- Hosted, OpenAI-compatible providers — e.g. Groq, OpenRouter, DeepSeek, Gemini, Z.ai, Mistral. The course code uses the OpenAI client, so you usually only need to change the `base_url`, the API key, and the model name.
- Open models via Hugging Face (e.g. Qwen, Llama) if you prefer hosted open-source models.
- Serve a model locally with [Ollama](https://ollama.com/), [vLLM](https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm), LM Studio, or anything else — no external API call at all, so regional blocks don't apply and you don't need a paid key. Most of these also expose an OpenAI-compatible endpoint, so the course code works with only a `base_url` change.
- Rent a GPU machine and serve the model there (e.g. with vLLM) if your own machine can't run the model you want. This gives you a private OpenAI-compatible endpoint to point the course code at — just remember to stop/delete the instance when you're done so you don't keep paying for it.
- A VPN also works if you just need to reach a provider that's blocked at the network level.

Anything with an OpenAI-compatible endpoint (or a locally served model) will work — pick whatever is available and convenient for you.

For a curated list of options, see [Awesome LLMs](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/awesome-llms.md) in the course repo, which collects OpenAI API alternatives and tools for running models locally.

question: How can I stop OpenAI 429 errors when I run notebooks that loop over lots of docs?
answer_llm:
Reduce the number of OpenAI calls before handling 429s.

What helps most:

- **Use retries with backoff**
  ```python
  from openai import OpenAI
  client = OpenAI(max_retries=5)
  ```
- **Lower concurrency**: keep thread/process pools small, around **2–3 workers**.
- **Cache outputs**: save embeddings/LLM responses to disk and reuse them on reruns.
- **Batch inputs**: send lists of texts in one call instead of looping one by one.
- **Use a cheaper/free provider**: `gpt-4o-mini` or Groq’s free tier.

Also, if the error is actually `insufficient_quota`, that’s **billing/quota**, not a rate limit. In that case you need to add credits or switch to a model/provider you can access.
answer_orig:
Free-tier and low-spend OpenAI accounts have per-minute and per-day request limits that are easy to blow through when a notebook loops over many documents. Reduce the number of API calls before you worry about handling the error after the fact.

- **Retry with backoff.** The `openai` Python client retries automatically with exponential backoff; raise the limit so transient 429s resolve themselves instead of crashing the run:

  ```python
  from openai import OpenAI

  client = OpenAI(max_retries=5)
  ```

- **Lower concurrency.** In a thread/process pool, keep the pool small (2-3 workers) so you stay under the per-minute cap. A larger pool finishes one batch fast and then fails on the next.
- **Cache results.** Write embeddings and LLM responses to disk (JSONL, pickle, or a vector DB) and reload on re-run. Re-executing a notebook should not re-call the API for inputs you already processed.
- **Batch where possible.** Group independent inputs into a single request (e.g. embed a list of texts in one call) instead of looping one-by-one.
- **Use a cheaper/free provider.** `gpt-4o-mini` is cheap enough for the whole course; Groq's free tier (`llama-3.3-70b-versatile`) has generous per-minute limits via the OpenAI-compatible endpoint.

If you already see `insufficient_quota`, that is a billing issue, not a rate limit — see [OpenAI: Error: RateLimitError: Error code: 429](openai-error-ratelimiterror-error-code-429).

question: What’s the easiest way to make retries work automatically for transient rate limits in the OpenAI client?
answer_llm:
The easiest way is to let the OpenAI Python client retry for you by increasing `max_retries`:

```python
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI(max_retries=5)
```

That gives transient 429 rate-limit errors time to resolve automatically with exponential backoff.

question: In agentic RAG, what exactly counts as a tool or function the model can use?
answer_llm:
In agentic RAG, a tool or function is **anything the model can call to get external help**.

Examples from the course context include:

- searching documents
- querying a database
- calling an API
- running a calculation

The key point is that the **model decides when to use the tool/function**.
answer_orig:
In the context of this course, tools and functions are closely related terms. Do not worry too much about the naming difference.

A tool/function is something the model can call when it needs external help, such as searching documents, querying a database, calling an API, or running a calculation.

The important idea is that in agentic RAG, the model can decide when to call a tool/function. In basic RAG, retrieval is usually a fixed step that happens before the model answers.

question: Are tools and functions different things in this course, or can I treat them the same?

- searching documents

question: Why do I get `AttributeError: 'str' object has no attribute 'output'` when I call the new Responses API with a model that isn’t from OpenAI?
answer_llm:
Because the new `Responses API` is OpenAI-specific. If you call `client.responses.create(...)` with a model from another provider, the returned object won’t have `.output` in the way OpenAI expects, so you can end up with:

`AttributeError: 'str' object has no attribute 'output'`

For non-OpenAI models, use the older chat-completions API instead and read:

```python
response.choices[0].message.content
```

Example:

```python
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="<provider-model>",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    tools=tools_schema,
)
return response.choices[0].message.content
```

You may also need to adapt the tools schema to match the provider’s expected format.

If you want, I can also show the exact fix for your provider (Groq, Gemini, Mistral, Anthropic, etc.).
answer_orig:
The new OpenAI Responses API (`client.responses.create(...)`, accessed via `response.output`) is OpenAI-specific. Other providers (Mistral, Groq, Gemini, etc.) don't implement it.

For non-OpenAI providers, use the chat-completions API and read `response.choices[0].message.content`:

```python
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="<provider-model>",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    tools=tools_schema,  # may need adapting per provider
)
return response.choices[0].message.content
```

You'll also have to adapt the tools schema to whatever shape your provider expects.

It is okay to use the older `chat.completions` API for homework or projects if your provider supports that interface better than the Responses API.

question: How do I fix the 400 error in Azure OpenAI that says tools[0].function is missing?
What should the get_weather_tool JSON look like so Azure OpenAI accepts it?
I keep getting an invalid_request_error about the function field in tools — what am I missing?

Why does my Azure OpenAI call fail with missing_required_parameter, and how should I define the weather tool?
Why do I get a 404 with toyaikit on Groq, and what should I switch to instead?
answer_llm:
You get the `404` because `toyaikit`’s `OpenAIResponsesRunner` / `OpenAIClient` uses OpenAI’s **Responses** endpoint (`responses.create`), and that endpoint is **OpenAI-only**. Groq doesn’t implement it, so the request fails.

You should switch to the **chat completions** classes instead, which use `chat.completions.create` and work with Groq and other OpenAI-compatible providers:

- `OpenAIChatCompletionsClient`
- `OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner`

If you want Anthropic instead of OpenAI, use:

- `AnthropicClient`
- `AnthropicMessagesRunner`
answer_orig:
If you get a `404` (or similar) when using toyaikit with Groq or another non-OpenAI provider, it's because `OpenAIResponsesRunner` / `OpenAIClient` call OpenAI's Responses endpoint (`responses.create`), which only OpenAI implements. Switch to the **chat completions** classes, which use the standard `chat.completions.create` endpoint that Groq and other OpenAI-compatible providers support.

### OpenAI or Groq (chat completions)

```python
import os
from openai import OpenAI
from toyaikit.tools import Tools
from toyaikit.llm import OpenAIChatCompletionsClient
from toyaikit.chat.runners import OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner

tools = Tools()
tools.add_tools(my_tools_object)   # functions need type hints + an Args: docstring

# OpenAI:
llm_client = OpenAIChatCompletionsClient(model="gpt-4o-mini", client=OpenAI())

# Groq (same runner, just point the OpenAI client at Groq's base URL):
groq_client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GROQ_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://api.groq.com/openai/v1",
)
llm_client = OpenAIChatCompletionsClient(model="llama-3.3-70b-versatile", client=groq_client)

runner = OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner(
    tools=tools,
    developer_prompt="You are a helpful assistant.",
    llm_client=llm_client,
)
result = runner.loop(prompt="What's the weather in Berlin?")
print(result.last_message)
```

### Anthropic

```python
from toyaikit.tools import Tools
from toyaikit.llm import AnthropicClient
from toyaikit.chat.runners import AnthropicMessagesRunner

tools = Tools()
tools.add_tools(my_tools_object)

# Reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from the environment automatically:
llm_client = AnthropicClient(model="claude-haiku-4-5")

runner = AnthropicMessagesRunner(
    tools=tools,
    developer_prompt="You are a helpful assistant.",
    llm_client=llm_client,
)
result = runner.loop(prompt="What's the weather in Berlin?")
print(result.last_message)
```

Notes:

- `runner.loop(prompt=...)` runs one turn programmatically and returns a result with `.last_message`, `.all_messages`, and `.tokens` — no chat interface needed. Use `runner.run()` only for the interactive Jupyter loop.
- Pass an explicit, current model id. For Anthropic, `claude-haiku-4-5` / `claude-sonnet-4-5` work; an outdated id can 404.
- For Groq you'll see a harmless `UnknownModelWarning: No pricing data...` — the call still succeeds, only cost calculation is skipped.

```python
llm_client = OpenAIChatCompletionsClient(model="gpt-4o-mini", client=OpenAI())

    api_key=os.getenv("GROQ_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://api.groq.com/openai/v1",
)
```

question: Why do I get a 402 error from OpenRouter when I call responses.create() in homework code, and what should I change so it works?
answer_llm:
You’re getting the 402 because OpenRouter is checking the **maximum possible output size** for `responses.create()`, and without a limit it can look too large for a free/low-limit key.

What to change:
- Add a **lower `max_output_tokens`** value to your `responses.create()` call.
- In the homework, `1024` is recommended and should be enough.

Example:

```python
response = client.responses.create(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    input=message_history,
    max_output_tokens=1024,
)
```

If you’re using `rag_helper.py`, add the same parameter in the `llm()` method.

If you’re using ToyAIKit:

```python
llm_client = OpenAIClient(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    extra_kwargs={"max_output_tokens": 1024},
)
```

Also make sure your `.env` is set to OpenRouter, for example:

```env
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-or-v1-...
OPENAI_BASE_URL=https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
OPENAI_MODEL=openai/gpt-oss-120b:free
```

If it still fails, check your OpenRouter credits/key limits and consider using a pinned model instead of `openrouter/free`.
answer_orig:
OpenRouter can return APIStatusError with code 402 when responses.create() is called without a reasonable max_output_tokens limit. This happens because OpenRouter bills/limits checks against the maximum possible output (which can be very large, around 65536 tokens), so a free or low-limit key can be rejected before the model runs. This is different from a direct OpenAI endpoint (which typically returns 429 for insufficient quota).

Fix

Pass a lower limit in your responses.create() call:

```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv
from openai import OpenAI

load_dotenv()

client = OpenAI()  # uses OPENAI_API_KEY and OPENAI_BASE_URL from .env

response = client.responses.create(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    input=message_history,
    max_output_tokens=1024,
)
```

For Module 1 homework with rag_helper.py, add the same parameter in the ``llm()`` method.

```python
response = client.responses.create(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    input=message_history,
    max_output_tokens=1024,
)
```

For Module 1 homework Q6 with ToyAIKit:

```python
from toyaikit.llm import OpenAIClient

llm_client = OpenAIClient(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    extra_kwargs={"max_output_tokens": 1024},
)
```

1024 is enough for homework answers; you can raise it later if needed.

If it still fails

1) Confirm your `.env` points at OpenRouter:

```env
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-or-v1-...
OPENAI_BASE_URL=https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
OPENAI_MODEL=openai/gpt-oss-120b:free
```

2) Check your OpenRouter key limit and remaining credits at https://openrouter.ai/settings/keys

3) Prefer a pinned model (for example `openai/gpt-oss-120b:free`) instead of `openrouter/free`, which can route to models with different limits.

Note: This behavior is different from OpenAI’s typical 429 handling for insufficient quota. If you still encounter issues after these steps, double-check the model and endpoint configuration to ensure the key is valid and has sufficient credits.

question: In the Module 1 homework, when they say RAG should point to the chunk index, does that mean I should run the search on the split docs instead of the original ones?
answer_llm:
Yes — point RAG at the **chunk index**, meaning you should run the search on the **split docs/chunks**, not the original full documents.

So instead of fitting/searching on `documents`, do it on `chunks`:

```python
index.fit(chunks)
```

That way the retriever returns relevant chunks and only those go into the prompt.
answer_orig:
It means you should build and search the index using the chunked documents, not the original full documents.

For example, if your original records are in `documents` and your split records are in `chunks`, fit the search index on `chunks`:

```python
index.fit(chunks)
```

not:

```python
index.fit(documents)
```

Then the RAG pipeline retrieves relevant chunks and puts only those chunks into the prompt. This is what reduces the amount of context sent to the model.

question: How should I count tokens for the Module 1 homework if I'm using a model other than OpenAI?
answer_llm:
For the Module 1 homework, if you use a non-OpenAI model/provider, count tokens from the **model response object’s usage fields**, not with `tiktoken`.

Examples:
- `response.usage.input_tokens`
- `response.usage.prompt_tokens`

Use whatever token-usage field your provider exposes. If it doesn’t expose usage, use that provider’s **native tokenizer** as an approximation.

Do **not** use `tiktoken` / `cl100k_base` as a generic tokenizer for Gemini, Mistral, Hugging Face, Groq, etc., because tokenization differs by model.
answer_orig:
For the current Module 1 homework, get the token count from the model response object.

For example, OpenAI-compatible clients usually return usage information on the response, such as `response.usage.input_tokens` or `response.usage.prompt_tokens`, depending on the API style.

If you use a non-OpenAI provider, check the provider's response object for its usage fields and adapt the code. Do not use `tiktoken` or `cl100k_base` as a generic tokenizer for Gemini, Mistral, Hugging Face, Groq, or other providers because tokenization differs by model.

If your provider does not expose token usage, use that provider's native tokenizer as an approximation. For multiple-choice homework questions, choose the closest option.

question: Why does the homework checker say my repo link is not returning 200, and what should I change?
answer_llm:
The checker sends a GET request to your submitted URL and expects an HTTP `200` response. If it gets `404`, `500`, or anything else, it usually means the repo isn’t publicly reachable as submitted.

What to change:
- Make the repo **public**
- Submit the **plain repo URL**, not the `.git` URL
  - Good: `https://github.com/you/repo`
  - Bad: `https://github.com/you/repo.git`
- Double-check for a **typo** in the link

Quick test: open the URL in an incognito/private browser window. If it loads there, the checker should be able to access it too.
answer_orig:
The submission checker fetches the URL you submit with a GET request and expects an HTTP `200` response. A non-200 status (404, 500, etc.) almost always means the link isn't publicly reachable as-is. Check the usual causes:

- **Private repo** — make the repository public so the checker can access it.
- **Trailing `.git`** — submit the plain repository URL (e.g. `https://github.com/you/repo`), not `https://github.com/you/repo.git`.
- **Typo in the URL** — paste the exact link.

A quick test: open the URL in a private/incognito browser window. If it loads for you there, it'll work for the checker too.

question: What exactly are embeddings in vector search, and why do they matter?
answer_llm:
Embeddings are numerical vectors that represent text or other non-numerical data in a way that preserves meaning and context.

Why they matter:
- Similar inputs produce similar vectors.
- That means you can measure semantic similarity mathematically.
- In vector search, this lets you find items that are meaningfully related, not just keyword-matching.

In short: embeddings turn meaning into numbers, so search can work by comparing vectors.
answer_orig: Embeddings refer to the process of converting non-numerical data into numerical data while preserving meaning and context. When similar non-numerical data is input into an embedding algorithm, it should yield similar numerical data. The proximity of these numerical values allows for the use of mathematical semantic similarity algorithms. Related concepts include the "vector space model" and "dimensionality reduction."

question: How does turning text into numbers keep the meaning and context?
answer_llm: Turning text into numbers via **embeddings** preserves meaning and context by mapping similar pieces of text to **similar numerical vectors**. That way, text with related semantics ends up close together in vector space, which lets algorithms measure **semantic similarity** mathematically.
answer_orig: Embeddings refer to the process of converting non-numerical data into numerical data while preserving meaning and context. When similar non-numerical data is input into an embedding algorithm, it should yield similar numerical data. The proximity of these numerical values allows for the use of mathematical semantic similarity algorithms. Related concepts include the "vector space model" and "dimensionality reduction."

question: Why do similar pieces of non-numerical data end up with close numerical values after embedding?
answer_llm: Because the embedding algorithm is designed to preserve meaning and context: similar non-numerical inputs are mapped to nearby points in vector space, so their numerical values end up close to each other.
answer_orig: Embeddings refer to the process of converting non-numerical data into numerical data while preserving meaning and context. When similar non-numerical data is input into an embedding algorithm, it should yield similar numerical data. The proximity of these numerical values allows for the use of mathematical semantic similarity algorithms. Related concepts include the "vector space model" and "dimensionality reduction."

question: How can those numbers be used to measure semantic similarity between texts?
answer_llm: Those numbers can be used as vector representations of the texts, and then compared with cosine similarity. If the vectors are normalized to unit length, their dot product gives a similarity score: higher values mean the texts are more semantically similar, while lower values mean they are less similar.
answer_orig: Embeddings refer to the process of converting non-numerical data into numerical data while preserving meaning and context. When similar non-numerical data is input into an embedding algorithm, it should yield similar numerical data. The proximity of these numerical values allows for the use of mathematical semantic similarity algorithms. Related concepts include the "vector space model" and "dimensionality reduction."

question: Is embedding the same thing as the vector space model or dimensionality reduction, or are they different concepts?
answer_llm:
They’re related, but not exactly the same.

- **Embeddings** are the general process of turning non-numerical data into numbers while preserving meaning and context.
- The **vector space model** is a related idea/framework where texts or objects are represented as vectors in a space so similarity can be measured mathematically.
- **Dimensionality reduction** is also related because embeddings often map high-dimensional or complex data into a lower-dimensional vector representation.

So: **embedding is the broader concept**, and vector space model / dimensionality reduction are **related concepts**, not identical synonyms.

question: Why did the lesson just use dot product for similarity instead of explicitly doing cosine similarity?
answer_llm:
Because the lesson assumed the embeddings were already normalized to unit length. In that case, the dot product is mathematically the same as cosine similarity, so there was no need to compute cosine explicitly.

In the homework, the embeddings are not guaranteed to be normalized by default, so you need to normalize them first if you want dot product to equal cosine similarity.
answer_orig:
In the lesson, `.dot(...)` was used under the assumption that the embeddings returned by the model (e.g., `model.encode(...)` from OpenAI) are already normalized to have unit length. In that case, the dot product is mathematically equivalent to cosine similarity.

In the homework, however, we use classic embeddings like TF-IDF + SVD, which are not normalized by default. This means that the dot product does not represent cosine similarity unless we manually normalize the vectors.

question: In the homework, why do we need to normalize the vectors before comparing them?
answer_llm:
We normalize the vectors so that the dot product is the same as cosine similarity.

In the homework, the embeddings are not normalized by default, so using `.dot(...)` directly would give you a plain dot product, not cosine similarity. Normalizing each vector to unit length makes the comparison correct.

question: When doing vector search, do I need to embed just the answer, just the question, or both together?
answer_llm:
There's no single right answer — it's something to experiment with on your dataset.

The course mentions three approaches:

- Embed the answer (`text`) only
- Embed the question only
- Embed `question + " " + text`

Often `question + text` works best, but it’s a bit longer and costs slightly more. The key is to compare them on your ground-truth set and choose whichever gives the best hit rate / MRR.
answer_orig:
There's no single right answer — it's an experiment to run on your dataset. The course shows three options:

- Embed the answer (`text`) only — works because the model captures semantic similarity between questions and their answers.
- Embed the question only — works because user queries look like the indexed questions.
- Embed `question + " " + text` — often the best, but produces longer input and slightly more cost.

Pick whichever gives the best hit rate / MRR on your ground-truth set. The course materials include a side-by-side comparison.

question: What does cosine similarity actually measure between two vectors?
answer_llm:
Cosine similarity measures the **angle between two vectors**, not their length. In other words, it tells you how **aligned** they are:

- **1** = same direction
- **0** = orthogonal, no similarity
- **-1** = opposite direction

So it’s basically a measure of **directional similarity** between two vectors.
answer_orig: Cosine similarity is a measure used to calculate the similarity between two non-zero vectors, often used in text analysis to determine how similar two documents are based on their content. This metric computes the cosine of the angle between two vectors, which are typically word counts or TF-IDF values of the documents. The cosine similarity value ranges from -1 to 1, where 1 indicates that the vectors are identical, 0 indicates that the vectors are orthogonal (no similarity), and -1 represents completely opposite vectors.

question: Why is cosine similarity commonly used for comparing documents in text analysis?
answer_llm:
Cosine similarity is commonly used because it measures how similar two documents are based on the **angle between their vector representations**, rather than their raw size.

That makes it useful in text analysis since documents can have very different lengths, but still be about the same topic. When documents are represented as word counts, TF-IDF, or embeddings, cosine similarity focuses on whether the vectors point in a similar direction, which reflects similar content.

question: How do you calculate cosine similarity for vectors like word counts or TF-IDF?
answer_llm:
For word count or TF-IDF vectors, cosine similarity is computed as the cosine of the angle between the two vectors. If the vectors are normalized to unit length, it’s just the dot product.

In practice:

1. Compute the dot product of the two vectors.
2. Divide by the product of their magnitudes:

\[
\cos(\theta) = \frac{A \cdot B}{\|A\|\|B\|}
\]

If the vectors are already normalized, then \(\|A\| = \|B\| = 1\), so cosine similarity reduces to:

\[
\cos(\theta) = A \cdot B
\]

It ranges from -1 to 1:
- 1 = identical direction
- 0 = orthogonal, no similarity
- -1 = opposite direction

The cosine similarity value ranges from -1 to 1, where 1 indicates that the vectors are identical, 0 indicates that the vectors are orthogonal (no similarity), and -1 represents completely opposite vectors.

- **1**: the vectors are **identical** in direction
- **0**: the vectors are **orthogonal** — meaning **no similarity**
- **-1**: the vectors are **completely opposite** direction

question: Why does cosine similarity turn into a dot product once the vectors are normalized?
answer_llm:
Cosine similarity is

\[
\cos(\theta)=\frac{a \cdot b}{\|a\|\|b\|}
\]

If both vectors are normalized to unit length, then \(\|a\|=\|b\|=1\). So the formula becomes:

\[
\cos(\theta)=a \cdot b
\]

That’s why, once vectors are normalized, cosine similarity turns into a simple dot product. The magnitudes no longer matter, only the angle between the vectors does.
answer_orig: Cosine similarity measures how aligned two vectors are, regardless of their magnitude. When all vectors (including the query) are normalized to unit length, their magnitudes no longer matter. In this case, cosine similarity is equivalent to simply taking the dot product between the query and each document embedding. This allows us to compute similarities efficiently using matrix multiplication.

question: If all embeddings have unit length, why can we just use matrix multiplication to get cosine scores?
answer_llm: Because cosine similarity between two vectors is just their dot product when both vectors are normalized to unit length. With unit-length embeddings, the magnitudes are all 1, so only the angle matters. That means you can compute cosine scores efficiently with matrix multiplication.

question: Why is cosine similarity basically the same as multiplying the embedding matrix by the query vector?
answer_llm:
Because if the vectors are normalized to unit length, cosine similarity becomes just the dot product.

So for a query vector `q` and an embedding matrix `X`:

- cosine similarity between `q` and each row of `X` = dot product of normalized vectors
- computing all dot products at once = matrix multiplication `X.dot(q)`

That’s why “cosine similarity” is basically the same as multiplying the embedding matrix by the query vector in this setup.

question: For Module 2, do I have to make a brand new GitHub repo, or can I just spin up another codespace from my current one?
answer_llm:
Just a new codespace — you do **not** need a brand new GitHub repo.

Create the Module 2 codespace from your existing repository, and keep it separate because the vector-search dependencies are heavier. Also, commit your work to the repo in case you later delete that codespace.
answer_orig:
Just a new codespace. A codespace is an environment (see *Can I run the course locally instead of Codespaces?*); you create it from your existing repository, so you don't need a new GitHub repo.

Use a separate codespace for Module 2 because the vector-search dependencies are fairly heavy. Keeping them isolated means you can simply stop or delete that codespace when you're done, rather than leaving the extra weight in your Module 1 environment. Setup is quick.

Since you may delete this codespace later, commit your work to your repository (see *What happens to code saved in Codespaces if I do not commit it?*).

question: Why does download.py get stuck at 0% when pulling model.onnx from HuggingFace, and what should I do instead?
answer_llm:
It can get stuck at 0% because the connection to HuggingFace’s CDN is slow or blocked.

What you should do instead:
- Download `model.onnx` directly in your browser from:
  https://huggingface.co/Xenova/all-MiniLM-L6-v2/resolve/main/onnx/model.onnx
- Save it to:
  `models/Xenova/all-MiniLM-L6-v2/model.onnx`

Once the file is there locally, `download.py` will detect it and skip downloading it again.

question: Why do my dot-product scores from NumPy and a Python loop not match exactly, even though the code should be the same?
answer_llm:
Because floating-point math isn’t exact.

`NumPy.dot()` and a Python loop both compute the same dot product, but they may add the terms in a different order. Since floating-point addition is not perfectly associative, the result can differ in the last few decimal places.

So the values are not really wrong — they’re just slightly different due to rounding. Compare them with a tolerance instead of exact equality, for example:

```python
np.allclose(scores, scores_loop, atol=1e-5)
```
answer_orig:
In [Lesson 4 (Vector Search)](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/02-vector-search/lessons/04-vector-search.md) we compute the scores two ways — `scores = X.dot(v_query)` and the equivalent for-loop — and they can come out slightly different. This is normal, it's just floating-point precision. If you compare them with `np.allclose(scores, scores_loop)` you might get `False`, even though both compute the same dot products. They just add the numbers up in a slightly different order, and floating-point addition isn't perfectly associative, so the results can differ in the last few decimal places.

Under the hood, `X.dot(v)` runs optimized BLAS code that sums in a different order than a sequential Python loop. The math is the same; only the rounding differs, so the values aren't really "wrong."

Just compare them with a small tolerance instead of expecting an exact match:

```python
np.allclose(scores, scores_loop, atol=1e-5)
```

question: I computed vector search both with matrix multiplication and with a for-loop, and the results differ by tiny amounts — is that a bug or just floating-point precision?
answer_llm:
It’s just floating-point precision, not a bug.

The matrix version (`X.dot(v_query)`) and the for-loop compute the same dot products, but they add numbers in a different order. Because floating-point addition isn’t perfectly associative, you can get tiny differences in the last few decimal places.

So:

- the results are effectively the same
- exact equality may fail
- use a tolerance check like:

```python

```

This is normal, it's just floating-point precision. If you compare them with `np.allclose(scores, scores_loop)` you might get `False`, even though both compute the same dot products. They just add the numbers up in a slightly different order, and floating-point addition isn't perfectly associative, so the results can differ in the last few decimal places.

Under the hood, `X.dot(v)` runs optimized BLAS code that sums in a different order than a sequential Python loop. The math is the same; only the rounding differs, so the values aren't really "wrong."

Just compare them with a small tolerance instead of expecting an exact match:

```python
np.allclose(scores, scores_loop, atol=1e-5)
```

- a Python loop sums sequentially

question: Why do I get "IndexError: list index out of range" when I try to access the best chunk after embedding my document chunks?
answer_llm:
The error usually means the number of embeddings doesn’t match the number of document chunks, so you end up trying to access a chunk index that doesn’t exist.

Make sure you create embeddings directly from the chunk list:

```python
contents = [chunk["content"] for chunk in chunks]
X = embedder.encode_batch(contents)
```

The number of rows in `X` should equal `len(chunks)`.


The number of rows in `X` should be equal to `len(chunks)`.

question: How do I fix the mismatch between embeddings and chunks so my vector search doesn’t break with an index error?
answer_llm:
The fix is to make sure your embeddings are created from the **same chunk list** you search over.

If you build chunks like this:

```python
chunks = [...]
```

then generate embeddings from `chunks`, not from the original documents:

```python
contents = [chunk["content"] for chunk in chunks]
X = embedder.encode_batch(contents)
```

The key rule is:

- `len(chunks)` must equal `X.shape[0]`

If those don’t match, your vector search can hit an `IndexError: list index out of range` when it tries to retrieve the best chunk.

Also, in the RAG pipeline, point the index at the **chunked documents**, not the original full documents:

```python
index.fit(chunks)
```

not

```python
index.fit(documents)
```

So the main fix is: **chunk first, embed those chunks, and index/search the chunks consistently**.

question: For Module 2 homework Q2, my cosine similarity doesn’t match any answer choice — could I be using the wrong embedding model?
answer_llm:
Yes — the most common issue is using the wrong embedding model.

For Module 2 Homework, you should **not** use `sentence-transformers`. The homework expects the lightweight ONNX `Embedder` from `embedder.py`:

```python
from embedder import Embedder
model = Embedder()
```

If you use a different model like `multi-qa-mpnet-base-dot-v1` or `all-mpnet-base-v2`, your cosine similarity can be different and not match any option.

Also double-check that you are:
- embedding the page’s **`content`** field,
- comparing it to the **query vector from Q1**,
- and using the fact that the `Embedder` returns **normalized vectors**, so the dot product is the cosine similarity.

If it’s still slightly off, choose the **closest option**.
answer_orig:
The most common cause is using a different embedding model than the homework specifies. Homework 2 tells you **not** to use `sentence-transformers` and to use the lightweight ONNX `Embedder` from `embedder.py` instead:

```python
from embedder import Embedder
model = Embedder()
```

Both approaches produce the same vectors for the same model, but if you embed with a different model (for example the `multi-qa-mpnet-base-dot-v1` model from the lessons, or `all-mpnet-base-v2`) you'll get a cosine value that isn't among the options.

Also check that:

- You're embedding the page's `content` field (not the filename or the whole dict).
- You're comparing against the query vector from Q1.
- The vectors are normalized — the `Embedder` returns normalized vectors, so the dot product is the cosine similarity directly.

If your value is still slightly off after using the right model, pick the closest option — small numerical differences are expected.

question: How do I set up Gemini, OpenAI, and Tavily API keys for the Kestra exercises?
answer_llm:
Set them in your terminal **before starting Kestra**.

For the Kestra exercises, Kestra expects secrets as environment variables prefixed with `SECRET_`, and the value must be **base64-encoded**. For Gemini, you also need the plain `GEMINI_API_KEY` for the AI Copilot.

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"                              # used by AI Copilot
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n $GEMINI_API_KEY | base64)             # used by the flows
export SECRET_OPENAI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "your-openai-api-key-here" | base64)  # required for flow 3
export SECRET_TAVILY_API_KEY=$(echo -n "your-tavily-api-key-here" | base64)  # required for web search (flows 3, 5, 6)
```

Then start or restart Kestra:

```bash
docker compose up -d
```

Inside a flow, reference the secret without the `SECRET_` prefix:

```yaml
{{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}
```

Remember not to commit your keys to Git.

question: Why does Kestra Copilot keep saying it can only help with creating flows, and what setting am I missing?
answer_llm:
Kestra Copilot says that when it **doesn’t get a valid Gemini API key**, so it falls back to a canned refusal.

The missing setting is the **plain `GEMINI_API_KEY` environment variable**. In Kestra OSS, the Copilot only supports Gemini and it does **not** use the base64-encoded `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY` that some flows use.

Set it before starting Kestra, then restart:

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"
docker compose up -d
```

Also make sure:
- the variable is set in the same shell where you run `docker compose up`
- the key is valid and not rate-limited

If needed, also export `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY` for the flows, but the Copilot specifically needs `GEMINI_API_KEY`.
answer_orig:
This message means the AI Copilot didn't get a valid Gemini API key, so it falls back to a canned refusal. In Kestra's Open Source edition the Copilot only supports Gemini, and it reads the **plain** `GEMINI_API_KEY` variable (not the base64-encoded `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY` that the flows use).

Make sure you exported the plain key before starting Kestra, then restart it:

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"
docker compose up -d
```

If it still fails, the key is usually missing, mistyped, or rate-limited:

- Confirm the variable is actually set in the shell you ran `docker compose up` from (`echo $GEMINI_API_KEY`).
- Generate a fresh key in [Google AI Studio](https://aistudio.google.com/app/apikey).
- If you've been running the agent/multi-agent flows a lot, you may have hit the free-tier quota (`429 Resource Exhausted`) — wait a minute and retry.

Note that the Copilot needs the **plain** `GEMINI_API_KEY` while the flows need `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY` — export both.

If it still refuses, check that:

- `GEMINI_API_KEY` is set in the same shell you use to start Kestra
- you restarted Kestra after exporting it

question: How can I check if the Copilot is failing because the Gemini key isn’t being picked up or because of quota issues?
answer_llm:
Check the error pattern:

- If the Copilot says it can only assist with creating Kestra flows, it’s usually **not seeing a valid plain `GEMINI_API_KEY`**.
- If the key is set correctly but requests still fail after repeated use, it may be a **quota/rate limit issue** like `429 Resource Exhausted`.

Quick checks:

1. Verify the plain key is set in the same shell you used to start Kestra:
   ```bash
   echo $GEMINI_API_KEY
   ```
2. Restart Kestra after exporting it:
   ```bash
   docker compose up -d
   ```
3. If it’s set and still failing, try a fresh Gemini key from Google AI Studio.
4. If you’ve been running the flows a lot, wait a minute and retry in case you hit the free-tier quota.

So in short:  
- **Missing/misread key** → check `echo $GEMINI_API_KEY` and restart Kestra.  
- **Quota issue** → look for `429 Resource Exhausted` and wait/retry.

question: Why does my Kestra flow say the Gemini key is missing even though I already set SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY?
answer_llm:
The usual reason is that the secret value was encoded incorrectly.

A common mistake is adding a trailing space or newline when base64-encoding the key, so Kestra receives the wrong decoded secret and reports it as missing/invalid. Use `echo -n` and quote the variable:

```bash
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "$GEMINI_API_KEY" | base64)
```

Also check these points:

- Restart Kestra after exporting the variable so it picks up the new environment:
  ```bash
  docker compose up -d
  ```
- In your flow, reference it **without** the `SECRET_` prefix:
  ```yaml
  {{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}
  ```
- Make sure you exported the variable in the same shell session used to start Kestra.

If needed, you can also place the variables in a `.env` file and start Kestra with:

```bash
docker compose --env-file ./.env -f ./docker-compose.yml up -d
```
answer_orig:
The most common cause is a corrupted base64 value. If you encode the key with a trailing space or newline, the decoded secret is wrong and authentication fails.

Use `echo -n` (which omits the trailing newline) and quote the value:

```bash
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "$GEMINI_API_KEY" | base64)
```

Other things to check:

- You must **restart** Kestra after exporting, so the container picks up the new variables: `docker compose up -d` (or `docker compose down && docker compose up -d`).
- Reference the secret in flows **without** the `SECRET_` prefix: `{{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}`.
- Export the variables in the same shell session you start Kestra from.

A cleaner alternative is to put the keys in a `.env` file and pass it explicitly:

```bash
docker compose --env-file ./.env -f ./docker-compose.yml up -d
```

question: Why can't we just use a notebook for this instead of an orchestrator like Kestra?
answer_llm:
Notebooks are great for learning and experimenting, but they’re not enough for production-style workflows.

You’d use an orchestrator like Kestra because real AI pipelines often need:

- scheduling
- retries
- monitoring
- secret management
- reliable chaining of multiple tasks

Kestra is also used in this module because it helps demonstrate the AI techniques the course is teaching, like:

- generating flows from natural language with AI Copilot
- grounding answers in real data with RAG
- using AI agents that decide which tools to call

So the point is not just to run code, but to show how AI fits into a production workflow.

If you want, I can also explain the difference between a notebook workflow and an orchestrated workflow with a simple example.
answer_orig:
Notebooks are great for learning and experimenting, but real AI workflows need more than a script that runs once: scheduling, retries, monitoring, secret management, and reliably chaining tasks together. That's what an orchestrator like Kestra provides.

In this module Kestra is also the vehicle for the AI techniques the course is teaching: AI Copilot to generate flows from natural language, RAG to ground responses in real data, and AI agents that decide which tools to call. The goal is to see how AI fits into production-style workflows, not just notebook cells.

Kestra's AI plugins also work with any major provider (OpenAI, Gemini, Anthropic, and more), so you can swap providers in a flow without changing anything else. See the [module intro](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/03-orchestration/lessons/01-intro.md) for the full motivation.

question: Do I really need to use Kestra for the whole LLM Zoomcamp, or is it only for the orchestration module?
answer_llm:
No — you only need Kestra for **Module 3: Orchestration**.

For the rest of the LLM Zoomcamp, you’re **not required** to use Kestra. And for the **capstone project**, you can use **Airflow, Prefect, Dagster, no orchestrator at all, or even a plain Python script**.
answer_orig:
No. Kestra is the orchestrator the course teaches in Module 3, but it is not a requirement beyond that module's homework.

For the **capstone project** you are not restricted in technology: you can use Airflow, Prefect, Dagster, or no orchestrator at all. A plain Python script that ingests and indexes your data is enough for full points on the ingestion-pipeline criterion (a Jupyter notebook with the same steps is worth 1 point instead of 2). See the [project guidelines](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/project.md) for details.

Kestra's AI plugins also work with any major provider (OpenAI, Gemini, Anthropic, and more), so you can swap providers in a flow without changing anything else — see the [supported providers list](https://kestra.io/plugins/plugin-ai/provider).

So you should pick whatever fits your project best; you do **not** need to use Kestra.

- **Vendor-specific plugins**: best when you need features unique to that vendor

question: Is there a way to build a Kestra flow without writing YAML by hand?
answer_llm: Yes. You can build a Kestra flow without writing YAML by hand using the **no-code form editor** or the **AI Copilot**. You can also generate flows with external editor-based tools like **Claude Code, Codex, or Kestra CTL**.
answer_orig:
Yes. You have three options that require no hand-written YAML or code:

- **The no-code form editor** builds a flow from a form instead of YAML. It is form-based (not drag-and-drop like n8n), so it suits people who don't want to edit YAML directly.
- **The AI Copilot** generates a flow from a natural-language description, and you can iterate with follow-up messages to add tasks or change the order. See the [AI Copilot lesson](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/03-orchestration/lessons/04-ai-copilot.md).
- **External agent skills** (Claude Code, Codex, Kestra CTL) let you generate flows and push them into Kestra from your editor without touching the Kestra UI.

Unlike Airflow, where business logic and orchestration logic are intertwined in Python, Kestra keeps the two separate: your Python script stays a plain script, and the workflow YAML just describes how and when to run it. This is closer to a cron job than to a traditional Airflow DAG.

For simple point-to-point automations (e.g. "post a Slack message when a YouTube video goes live"), a tool like Zapier or n8n may be simpler and cheaper. Kestra's advantage is observability, retries, and handling more complex or technical workflows in one place.

question: Why does ChatGPT sometimes invent Kestra plugin fields like bucket or name instead of the real ones?
answer_llm:
Because a generic AI assistant isn’t grounded in the exact Kestra plugin schema for your running version, it may “hallucinate” plausible field names like `bucket` or `name` that don’t actually exist. Kestra plugin properties are version-specific, so the real field might be something else entirely, like `to` for GCS upload or `destinationTable` for BigQuery load.

To avoid this:
- Check the official Kestra plugin docs for your version
- Use Kestra’s built-in AI Copilot, which is grounded in the current schema
- Validate the generated YAML in your Kestra environment

So the short answer is: ChatGPT invents them because it’s guessing from patterns, not reading the live Kestra schema.
answer_orig:
A general AI (e.g., ChatGPT) isn't grounded in Kestra's plugin schemas for your running version, so it can surface plausible but invalid property names (such as `bucket`/`name`) instead of the real, version-specific property. For example:

**GCS Upload** — invented `bucket`/`name` props vs. the real `to` property:

```yaml
# Incorrect (invented props)
- id: upload_to_gcs
  type: "io.kestra.plugin.gcp.gcs.Upload"
  bucket: "my-bucket"
  name: "path/to/file"

# Correct
- id: upload_to_gcs
  type: "io.kestra.plugin.gcp.gcs.Upload"
  to: "gs://my-bucket/path/to/file"
```

**BigQuery LoadFromGcs** — split `projectId`/`dataset`/`table` vs. the real `destinationTable`:

```yaml
# Incorrect (pseudo-split properties)
- id: load_to_bq
  type: "io.kestra.plugin.gcp.bigquery.LoadFromGcs"
  projectId: "my-project"
  dataset: "my_dataset"
  table: "my_table"

# Correct
- id: load_to_bq
  type: "io.kestra.plugin.gcp.bigquery.LoadFromGcs"
  destinationTable: "my-project.my_dataset.my_table"
```

To avoid this:

- Cross-check generated YAML against the official plugin docs: https://kestra.io/plugins/plugin-gcp
- Use Kestra's built-in AI Copilot, which is grounded in the current plugin schema for your running version.
- Validate and test your YAML in your Kestra environment to ensure it parses and runs as expected.

Note that plugin properties can vary by plugin version; what's correct in one release may be invalid in another. If you share a snippet, we can help verify it against the docs.

**BigQuery LoadFromGcs** — split `projectId`/`dataset`/`table` vs. the real `destinationTable`:

```yaml
  type: "io.kestra.plugin.gcp.bigquery.LoadFromGcs"
  projectId: "my-project"
  dataset: "my_dataset"
  table: "my_table"

# Correct
- id: load_to_bq
  type: "io.kestra.plugin.gcp.bigquery.LoadFromGcs"
  destinationTable: "my-project.my_dataset.my_table"
```

To avoid this:

- Cross-check generated YAML against the official plugin docs: https://kestra.io/plugins/plugin-gcp
- Use Kestra's built-in AI Copilot, which is grounded in the current plugin schema for your running version.
- Validate and test your YAML in your Kestra environment to ensure it parses and runs as expected.

Note that plugin properties can vary by plugin version; what's correct in one release may be invalid in another. If you share a snippet, we can help verify it against the docs.

question: Why do we even track token usage in Kestra flows, is it mainly about cost?
answer_llm:
Yes — mainly, but not only.

In Kestra flows, token usage is tracked because it maps directly to what LLM providers bill you for. So it helps with:

- **Cost control**: budget and spot expensive runs early
- **Prompt optimization**: see where prompts or outputs use too many tokens
- **Guardrails**: prevent long-running or looping flows from racking up a big bill

So it’s about cost, but also about making prompts more efficient and keeping workflows safe.
answer_orig:
Token usage is tracked because it maps directly to what LLM providers charge you for, and Kestra workflows run LLM calls on a schedule or in loops where costs add up fast. The main reasons:

- **Cost control**: tokens are the unit providers bill by, so tracking usage lets you budget, forecast, and spot cost spikes before they get expensive.
- **Prompt optimization**: seeing how many tokens each prompt and response consumes helps you tighten prompts and trim output without losing quality.
- **Guardrails**: in long-running or looping flows, usage monitoring lets you set thresholds and alerts so a runaway task doesn't quietly rack up a large bill.

In practice, each AI task in a Kestra flow can capture usage from the provider's response (for example `usage.total_tokens`), and you can surface those numbers in logs, metrics, or dashboards to keep an eye on spend per run.

question: How does watching token counts help when I’m trying to reduce LLM bills?
answer_llm:
Watching token counts helps you estimate and control usage, because LLM cost is usually tied directly to how many tokens you send and receive.

A few practical points from the context:

- Token counts are how providers measure usage on the API response.
- If you reduce prompt length, extra instructions, or unnecessary chat history, you usually reduce tokens and cost.
- With OpenAI, the API may count a few extra “chat formatting” tokens beyond what you see in plain text, so API-reported usage is the number to watch.
- For non-OpenAI providers, use the token usage reported by that provider’s response object, since tokenization differs across models.

So in short: tracking token counts tells you where your bill is coming from and whether prompt trimming is actually saving money.

question: How do I get rid of the Docker error saying a path under /tmp isn’t shared from the host?
answer_llm:
This error means Docker Desktop isn’t allowed to access the host path you’re trying to mount, often something under `/tmp` like `/tmp/kestra-wd`.

Fix it by sharing that path in Docker Desktop:

1. Open **Docker Desktop**
2. Click the **gear icon** for **Settings**
3. Go to **Resources > File Sharing**
4. Click **+**
5. Add `/tmp` or the specific path, like `/tmp/kestra-wd`
6. Click **Apply & restart**

Then rerun:

```bash
docker compose up -d
```
answer_orig:
This error means Docker Desktop isn't allowed to access the host path the container is trying to mount (commonly `/tmp/kestra-wd` when running Kestra). Add the path to Docker Desktop's file sharing list:

1. Open Docker Desktop.
2. Click the gear icon (Settings) in the top right.
3. Go to **Resources > File Sharing**.
4. Click the **+** (Add) button.
5. Enter `/tmp` (or the specific path like `/tmp/kestra-wd`) and press Enter.
6. Click **Apply & restart**.

Once Docker restarts, rerun `docker compose up -d` to start Kestra.

question: Why am I getting a 404 NOT_FOUND from Gemini in the Kestra workflow, and how can I fix it?
answer_llm:
You’re getting a `404 NOT_FOUND` because the Gemini model id used in the Kestra flow is no longer available for your account, and model availability can change over time.

How to fix it:
1. Open the affected YAML file.
2. Replace the current `modelName` with a Gemini model that is currently available to you.

Example:

```yaml
provider:
  type: io.kestra.plugin.ai.provider.GoogleGemini
  modelName: gemini-3.5-flash
  apiKey: "{{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}"
```

If you use `docker-compose.yml`, update the equivalent `model-name` setting there too.
Then rerun the flow. If the change was in Docker Compose config, restart the services.

Also check the official Gemini model list for current valid model ids:
https://ai.google.dev/gemini-api/docs/models

Don’t assume the model was permanently shut down — the `404` can be temporary or account-specific.
answer_orig:
Model availability changes over time and can vary by account. If a course workflow returns a `404 NOT_FOUND` saying that its Gemini model is unavailable, replace the model id in the affected YAML file with one currently available to you. For example, in a Kestra flow:

```yaml
provider:
  type: io.kestra.plugin.ai.provider.GoogleGemini
  modelName: gemini-3.5-flash
  apiKey: "{{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}"
```

In `docker-compose.yml`, update the equivalent `model-name` setting. Then rerun the flow, or restart the Docker Compose services if you changed their configuration.

Check the [official Gemini model list](https://ai.google.dev/gemini-api/docs/models) for current model ids. Do not assume the original model was permanently shut down based on a temporary `404`; select a model that the API makes available to your account.
